perturbation.original_text: Representatives ” means, with respect to a Party, such Party’s wholly owned subsidiaries, directors, officers, employees, consultants, accountants, financial and legal advisors and, with and subject to the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, any actual or potential sources of debt financing (including any affiliate of any financial advisor acting in such capacity and their counsel) and other representatives which are identified to the Disclosing Party and who shall be subject to confidentiality obligations at least as stringent as a Receiving Party hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Representatives ” means, with respect to a Party, such Party’s directors, officers, and employees only. Consultants, accountants, financial and legal advisors, and potential sources of debt financing are explicitly excluded from the definition of "Representatives" for the purpose of this agreement, and must be treated as third parties.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by narrowing the definition of 'Representatives'. Section 2(a) discusses the responsibility of the Receiving Party to ensure their Representatives hold Confidential Information in accordance with the agreement. By excluding consultants, accountants, financial and legal advisors in the modified definition, it creates ambiguity as to whether the Receiving Party is liable for any confidential information leaked by the financial and legal advisors. This contradicts Section 2(c) and the responsibilities of the Receiving Party, as the definition does not clarify if those parties should be subjected to the same standard of care.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Obligations of Confidentiality Each Party recognizes and acknowledges the competitive value and confidential nature of the Confidential Information and the damage that could result to the Disclosing Party if Confidential Information contained therein is disclosed to any person. As a condition to and in consideration of Confidential Information being provided to the Receiving Party and its Representatives, each Receiving Party undertakes and agrees as follows: (a) to hold and cause its Representatives to hold Confidential Information provided hereunder now or in the future in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement and not to disclose or permit it to be disclosed to any person, firm or company other than the Receiving Party’s Representatives who need to know such information for the Permitted Purpose; 1 (b) only to use the Confidential Information for the Permitted Purpose and not for any other purpose; (c) to ensure that each Representative to whom disclosure of Confidential Information is made by the Receiving Party is fully aware in advance of the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement and to take full responsibility and remain fully liable for any actions or omissions of its Representatives that are not in accordance with this Agreement; and (d) to keep confidential and not reveal to any person, firm or company (other than Representatives) the fact that Confidential Information has been made available in connection with the Permitted Purpose, that discussions or negotiations are taking place or have taken place between the Parties concerning a potential Transaction between the Parties, including the status of such discussions or the termination of such discussions or negotiations, or any opinions or view with respect to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the definition of 'Representatives' to exclude consultants, accountants, financial and legal advisors, and potential sources of debt financing. The contradicted text (Section 2(c)) states the Receiving Party is fully responsible for the actions and omissions of its Representatives. By excluding these parties, the Receiving Party's liability for their actions regarding confidential information is now ambiguous, creating a contradiction with the original intent of Section 2(c) which was to hold the receiving party liable for all Representatives.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that access to Limited Access Confidential Information shall be granted only to attorney Representatives who have been pre-approved in writing (which may be by email) by the Disclosing Party (“ Designated Representatives ”). Without limiting the confidentiality obligations set forth in Section 2, the Receiving Party shall ensure that Limited Access Confidential Information (including any notes, extracts, summaries, copies or tangible embodiments thereof) is not disclosed to any Representative other than Designated Representatives; it being understood that the Designated Representatives can provide the Receiving Party with written or oral legal advice or analyses based on the review of such Limited Access Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that access to Limited Access Confidential Information shall be granted only to attorney Representatives who have been pre-approved in writing (which may be by email) by the Disclosing Party (“ Designated Representatives ”). Notwithstanding the confidentiality obligations set forth in Section 2, the Receiving Party is permitted to share Limited Access Confidential Information (including any notes, extracts, summaries, copies or tangible embodiments thereof) with any Representative, provided that the Receiving Party deems it necessary for the Permitted Purpose, and the Receiving Party ensures such representative will only provide written or oral legal advice based on the review of such Limited Access Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by allowing the Receiving Party to share Limited Access Confidential Information with any representative, contrasting with the original text, where that action was only permitted with Designated Representatives that were pre-approved by the Disclosing Party. This contradicts the overall intent of Section 4, which is to limit the distribution of highly sensitive information. By giving the Receiving Party more control without pre-approval from the disclosing party, less stringent standards may be imposed. This introduces uncertainty about the level of confidentiality applied to sensitive information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) The Parties acknowledge and agree that certain highly-sensitive Confidential Information may in the reasonable discretion of the Disclosing Party be designated “Attorneys Eyes’ Only” (collectively, “ Limited Access Confidential Information ”). The Receiving Party agrees that access to Limited Access Confidential Information shall be granted only to attorney Representatives who have been pre-approved in writing (which may be by email) by the Disclosing Party (“ Designated Representatives ”). Notwithstanding the confidentiality obligations set forth in Section 2, the Receiving Party is permitted to share Limited Access Confidential Information (including any notes, extracts, summaries, copies or tangible embodiments thereof) with any Representative, provided that the Receiving Party deems it necessary for the Permitted Purpose, and the Receiving Party ensures such representative will only provide written or oral legal advice based on the review of such Limited Access Confidential Information. Without limiting Section 2(c), the Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Designated Representatives. With respect to Limited Access Confidential Information (including any notes, copies or tangible embodiments thereof), the Receiving Party’s obligations under Section 2 shall apply in perpetuity (unless one or more of the exceptions set forth in subsections (i), (ii) or (iii) of Section 3 applies). Upon termination of this Agreement or the request of the Disclosing Party, all notes, extracts, summaries, copies or tangible embodiments of Limited Access Confidential Information shall be permanently deleted and not retained by the Receiving Party, without exception, other than attorney work product and analyses based on the review of Limited Access Confidential Information by Designated Representatives that the Receiving Party stores on backup disks or in backup storage facilities automatically produced in the ordinary course of business consistent with past practice or by any applicable law, regulation, court order or legal process.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original text, allowing the Receiving Party to share Limited Access Confidential Information with 'any' representative if deemed necessary, whereas the original and contradicted text state that such information can 'only' be shared with 'Designated Representatives' pre-approved by the Disclosing Party. This creates a direct conflict and inconsistency, as it broadens the scope of permissible recipients beyond the pre-approved list, undermining the intended restriction on highly sensitive information and leading to an in-text contradiction as defined by the 'Inconsistencies' perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that neither Party nor its Representatives shall have any liability to the other 3 Party or its Representatives resulting from the use of the Confidential Information (as permitted pursuant to this Agreement) other than as may be set forth in a definitive agreement between the Parties concerning the Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that neither Party nor its Representatives shall have any liability to the other Party or its Representatives resulting from the use of the Confidential Information (as permitted pursuant to this Agreement), regardless of whether it is set forth in a definitive agreement between the Parties concerning the Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the 'other than as may be set forth in a definitive agreement' clause, potentially contradicting Section 8, Remedies. By removing this conditional clause and adding a new negative, the modified text suggests there is absolutely no liability for the use of confidential information, this contradicts Section 8, which permits the Disclosing Party to seek injunctions, specific performance, or other equitable relief, implying that damages may not be an adequate remedy for breaches of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Remedies Without limiting other remedies that may be available to the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party agrees that damages may not be an adequate remedy for any breach (whether actual or threatened) of the provisions of this Agreement and that accordingly, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek the remedies of injunction, specific performance or other equitable relief.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the conditional clause 'other than as may be set forth in a definitive agreement' from the original text and adds 'regardless of whether it is set forth in a definitive agreement'. This creates a direct contradiction with the 'Remedies' section. The 'Remedies' section states that 'damages may not be an adequate remedy for any breach...and that accordingly, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek the remedies of injunction, specific performance or other equitable relief.' The original text allows for liability as described in a definitive agreement. The changed text eliminates any liability. This creates a conflict because Section 8 allows for remedies, indicating potential liability while the modified text states there is no liability. Therefore, it fits the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' where 'different sections of a contract provide conflicting obligations.'

perturbation.original_text: Unless and until a written definitive agreement concerning the Transaction has been executed, neither Party nor any of its Representatives will have any legal obligation or liability to the other Party of any kind whatsoever with respect to the Transaction, whether by virtue of this Agreement or any other written or oral expression with respect to the Transaction or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless and until a written definitive agreement concerning the Transaction has been executed, neither Party will have any legal obligation or liability to the other Party of any kind whatsoever with respect to the Transaction, whether by virtue of this Agreement or any other written or oral expression with respect to the Transaction or otherwise. Representatives, however, are fully liable and obligated to act in good faith throughout the transaction.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces a contradiction by holding the Representatives liable and obligated to act in good faith throughout the transaction, although the parties themselves have no legal obligation until an agreement is executed. This directly contrasts with Section 2, Obligations of Confidentiality, where both parties are responsible for the actions of their Representatives. The newly introduced liability for Representatives alone could cause internal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Obligations of Confidentiality Each Party recognizes and acknowledges the competitive value and confidential nature of the Confidential Information and the damage that could result to the Disclosing Party if Confidential Information contained therein is disclosed to any person. As a condition to and in consideration of Confidential Information being provided to the Receiving Party and its Representatives, each Receiving Party undertakes and agrees as follows: (a) to hold and cause its Representatives to hold Confidential Information provided hereunder now or in the future in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement and not to disclose or permit it to be disclosed to any person, firm or company other than the Receiving Party’s Representatives who need to know such information for the Permitted Purpose; 1 (b) only to use the Confidential Information for the Permitted Purpose and not for any other purpose; (c) to ensure that each Representative to whom disclosure of Confidential Information is made by the Receiving Party is fully aware in advance of the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement and to take full responsibility and remain fully liable for any actions or omissions of its Representatives that are not in accordance with this Agreement; and (d) to keep confidential and not reveal to any person, firm or company (other than Representatives) the fact that Confidential Information has been made available in connection with the Permitted Purpose, that discussions or negotiations are taking place or have taken place between the Parties concerning a potential Transaction between the Parties, including the status of such discussions or the termination of such discussions or negotiations, or any opinions or view with respect to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction. Originally, neither party nor its representatives had legal obligations before a written agreement. The change makes Representatives liable for acting in good faith. This contradicts the 'Obligations of Confidentiality' section, which states each party is responsible for the actions of its Representatives. The changed text creates a direct conflict by imposing liability on representatives independent of the parties' obligations, conflicting with the original agreement that parties are responsible for their representatives. Thus, it creates an in-text contradiction as defined under 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Without limiting the foregoing, the following provisions shall survive termination of this Agreement: Sections 1-5 and 7-10 and Section 12.
perturbation.changed_text: Without limiting the foregoing, the following provisions shall survive termination of this Agreement: Sections 1-3 and 7-10 and Section 12.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces an in-text contradiction by removing Sections 4 and 5 from the list of sections that survive the termination of the Agreement. Section 4 discusses the return of confidential information, including Limited Access Confidential Information, in perpetuity. By removing it from the surviving sections, it implies that Section 4 terminates with the agreement. This contradicts Section 4(b), where there is mention of perpetuity for the Receiving Party, under Section 2, regarding obligations for Limited Access Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) The Parties acknowledge and agree that certain highly-sensitive Confidential Information may in the reasonable discretion of the Disclosing Party be designated “Attorneys Eyes’ Only” (collectively, “ Limited Access Confidential Information ”). The Receiving Party agrees that access to Limited Access Confidential Information shall be granted only to attorney Representatives who have been pre-approved in writing (which may be by email) by the Disclosing Party (“ Designated Representatives ”). Without limiting the confidentiality obligations set forth in Section 2, the Receiving Party shall ensure that Limited Access Confidential Information (including any notes, extracts, summaries, copies or tangible embodiments thereof) is not disclosed to any Representative other than Designated Representatives; it being understood that the Designated Representatives can provide the Receiving Party with written or oral legal advice or analyses based on the review of such Limited Access Confidential Information. Without limiting Section 2(c), the Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Designated Representatives. With respect to Limited Access Confidential Information (including any notes, copies or tangible embodiments thereof), the Receiving Party’s obligations under Section 2 shall apply in perpetuity (unless one or more of the exceptions set forth in subsections (i), (ii) or (iii) of Section 3 applies). Upon termination of this Agreement or the request of the Disclosing Party, all notes, extracts, summaries, copies or tangible embodiments of Limited Access Confidential Information shall be permanently deleted and not retained by the Receiving Party, without exception, other than attorney work product and analyses based on the review of Limited Access Confidential Information by Designated Representatives that the Receiving Party stores on backup disks or in backup storage facilities automatically produced in the ordinary course of business consistent with past practice or by any applicable law, regulation, court order or legal process.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that Sections 1-5 survived termination, which included Section 4. Section 4 discusses confidential information and obligations in perpetuity. The changed text removes Sections 4 and 5 from the list of surviving sections. The contradicted text, Section 4(b), specifically mentions that 'the Receiving Party’s obligations under Section 2 shall apply in perpetuity'. By removing Section 4 from the surviving sections, the changed text implies Section 4 and its perpetual obligations do not survive termination, directly contradicting Section 4(b)'s perpetuity clause. This is an inconsistency that directly conflicts with the contradicted text, making enforcement unclear, and according to the explanation given, is an in-text contradiction.
